segments.s130-g41: به خواب اندر آمد is a poetic way to say “came into sleep / fell asleep”: به + خواب marks motion into the state ‘sleep’ (به = towards/into), اندر is an older preposition meaning ‘in/within’, and آمد is simple past 3rd‑singular ‘came’. بد روزگار is a noun phrase ‘bad fate/time’ with the adjective بد placed before روزگار; together they function as the subject/what has come into sleep.
segments.s130-g42: ز خوبی و از داد آموزگار strings two causal/partitive prepositional phrases: ز and از both mean ‘from’/‘because of’ in poetic diction and introduce the sources or causes. خوبی is ‘goodness’; و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’; داد modifies آموزگار (a genitive/attributive relation) so داد آموزگار reads ‘the teacher/instructor of justice’ or ‘moral instructor’. The whole segment gives the origin/reason connected to the previous clause.
segments.s130-g43: به رنجش گرفتار is a state expression: به + رنجش ‘into resentment/anger’ and گرفتار (a participle/adjective) ‘caught/afflicted’ yields ‘caught by/afflicted with resentment’. دیوان is the subject in the plural/collective sense ‘the divan/court/officials’, and بدند is an archaic past plural copula/verb meaning ‘they were’ (equivalent to modern بودند).
segments.s130-g44: ز introduces the cause/means ‘by/from’; بادافره is a compound adjective ‘wind‑borne, wind‑tossed’ describing the following possessive phrase او غریوان (literally ‘his bosom/collar’); together the phrase z + X + Y بدند means ‘they were Y because of/from his X’. بدند again is the past plural ‘they were’. Note the poetic order: modifier (بادافره) precedes the possessor (او) and the body‑noun (غریوان).
segments.s130-g1: بیامد is a simple past (3rd singular) 'he came'; سوی پارس is a prepositional phrase 'toward Pars' that follows the verb in this poetic order. کاووس is the proper name 'Kavus' and کی is an archaic relative/connector (classical form of که) meaning 'who/that' or here effectively marking the subject 'Kavus' as the one who does what follows.
segments.s130-g2: جهانی 'the world' is the direct theme; به شادی 'with joy' is an adverbial phrase. نوافگند is a compound/past verbal form (نو- + افگند/گند) meaning 'made anew, renewed, caused to be' and functions as the main verb. پی at the end is an adverbial/temporal particle ('after/thereupon' or 'then/continually' in older Persian) that modifies the action of the verb.
segments.s130-g3: بیاراست is a past (3rd singular) verb 'he adorned/arranged'; تخت is the direct object 'the throne'. و simply links clauses. بگسترد is another past verb 'he spread/extended' and داد here is the noun 'justice' (not a past-tense verb), so the clause means 'he spread justice.'
segments.s130-g4: به شادی و خوردن forms a coordinated object ('joy and eating/feasting') introduced by the preposition به 'into/with'. دل is 'heart' and اندر is the classical preposition 'in/within'. نهاد here is the past of نهادن ('he placed/put'), so the phrase means 'he put joy and feasting into the heart(s)'.
segments.s130-g5: فرستاد is past 'he sent/dispatched'. هر سو is 'each direction/side' (هر modifies سو). یکی پهلوان is an indefinite numeral phrase 'one champion' (i.e. 'a single champion' or 'a champion each'), with the numeral coming before the noun as typical in Persian.
segments.s130-g6: جهاندار، بیدار و روشن‌روان are three coordinating adjectives/appositions describing that champion: جهاندار (literally 'world‑holding' = mighty/sovereign), بیدار 'awake/alert', روشن‌روان 'bright‑souled/cheerful'. The adjectives follow the noun they modify, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s130-g7: به introduces a destination/list: مرو، نشاپور، بلخ و هری are place names (Merv, Nishapur, Balkh, Herat). In classical Persian a sequence of places after به indicates the places to which something is sent or given.
segments.s130-g8: فرستاد repeats the past verb 'he sent'; بر هر سویی uses the preposition بر with هر سویی to mean 'toward every side' (بر here = 'toward/to'). لشکری is an adjectival/collective noun 'an army/troops' used here as the object (lit. 'he sent an army to every direction'). Word order places the direction phrase before the noun لشکری for emphasis, a poetic ordering.
segments.s130-g25: دو خانه دگر = a noun phrase 'two other houses' (دو numeral + خانه noun + دگر 'other'). ز آبگینه is an ablative/partitive prepositional phrase 'of/from glass' placed before the verb. بساخت is an archaic past third-person singular of ساختن ('he built/made'); the prosthetic ب- is common in poetic diction.
segments.s130-g26: This clause describes the ornament: زبرجد (the gem, used instrumentally) + به هر جایش اندر (adverbial phrase 'in/at every place') followed by the verb نشاخت (an archaic/poetic past verb meaning 'he inlaid/laid/set into place'). Word order places the material (زبرجد) and the locative phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s130-g27: چنان ساخت = 'he made/built it thus' (چنان adverb modifying ساخت). جای خرام و خورش is a compound noun phrase 'a place of خرام (strutting/proud gait, here a festive procession or display) and خورش (food/feasting)'; modifiers follow the head جای in this poetic ordering.
segments.s130-g28: که introduces a result/relative clause 'so that/that'. تن یابد is a verbal phrase where یابد is classical/poetic present/subjunctive/optative 'may find/attain' (expressing result or purpose) and تن = 'the body'. از خوردنی پرورش = 'nourishment/growth from the edible (food)' with از marking source.
segments.s130-g29: دو خانه = 'two houses'. ز بهر سلیح is a purpose/genitive phrase 'for the sake of weapons' (ز بهر = 'for/for the purpose of' + سلیح 'weapons'). نبرد here is a noun 'war/battle' used to qualify purpose (for war/for battle).
segments.s130-g30: بفرمو is an archaic/honorific verb form (variant of بفرمود/بفرما) meaning 'he commanded/ordered' or 'he had (it) done'; کز introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'that/from which/that which'. نقره خام کرد = 'he made (them) of raw silver', where کرد is the past of کردن 'to make/do'.
segments.s130-g31: یکی کاخ زرین = 'one golden palace' (یکی numeral + کاخ noun + زرین adjective). ز بهر نشست = a purpose phrase 'for seating/for a seat' (بهر = for, نشست here nominal 'a seat/sitting, i.e. a place to sit or an audience/seat').
segments.s130-g32: برآورد is a past verb 'he brought up/erected/raised' (in classical Persian verbs of bringing/raising often use برآورد). و joins the two actions. بالاش داده 'gave it above/up' = he added height/raised it upwards, and دو شست is a small measure 'two thumbs/spans' (شست = thumb used here as a unit of measure), so the clause says he lifted/raised it by two spans.
segments.s130-g9: جهانی is the subject 'the world'. پر از داد is an adjectival phrase 'filled with justice' where پر takes از to mark the content. شد is the past verb 'became' (classical past/perfective). یکسره is an adverb 'entirely/completely' placed after the verb for emphasis, so the whole clause reads 'the world became completely full of justice.'
segments.s130-g10: همی is a classical narrative particle with continuous/imperfective force here ('kept on' / 'then' / 'so'), followed by روی 'face' (used idiomatically with turning). برتافت is the main past verb meaning 'turned away/away he turned' (an archaic/poetic verbal form). گرگ 'wolf' is the subject, از is the preposition 'from', and بره 'lamb' is the object — together: 'the wolf turned away from the lamb.'
segments.s130-g11: ز بس is a poetic causal phrase 'because of so much' (ز as a causal/from + بس 'so much/enough'). گنج 'treasure' and زیبای (here representing زیبایی) 'beauty' are listed, joined by و, and فرهی 'noble/cultured' qualifies the quality — the phrase sets up the cause for what follows.
segments.s130-g12: پری, دد and دام are nouns 'fairy, enemy, and trap.' گشتش is the past of گشتن with enclitic pronoun -ش 'for him/it', meaning 'became for him' or 'turned into'. رهی here indicates the idea of a way/road or the course of his path; together the clause says his way/path became full of fairies, foes and snares.
segments.s130-g13: مهان 'visitors/guests' is the noun subject. پیش کاووس 'in the presence of Kavus' (پیش = before/in front of + proper name). کهتر شدند is the compound predicate: کهتر 'inferior/lesser' + شدند 'became' — 'the guests became inferior/humbled before Kavus.'
segments.s130-g14: همه 'all' + تاجدارنش ('his crown-bearers' — تاجداران with possessive -ش) is the subject phrase. لشکر شد ند uses لشکر 'army/host' as predicate noun and شدند 'became', so 'all his crowned ones became an army' (i.e., the crowned nobles became his host/forces).
segments.s130-g15: جهان پهلوانی is a genitive noun phrase 'the world's heroism / the championry of the world' (جهان + پهلوانی). به رستم marks the recipient 'to Rostam' (preposition به), and سپرد is the past verb 'entrusted/handed over' — 'he entrusted the world's champion/heroism to Rostam.'
segments.s130-g16: همه روزگار 'all the days/time' is the object. بهی 'good/excellent' qualifies روزگار ('days good'), ز indicates the cause 'because of/from' (here 'because of him'), و is the conjunction 'and', and شمرد is the past verb 'counted/ reckoned' — together: 'he counted all the days good because of him.'
segments.s130-g17: یکی here is an indefinite numeral meaning “one / a certain” placed before the noun; خانه کرد is a past tense verb phrase (کرد = ‘made/built’) with an omitted subject (understood from context). اندر introduces a locative phrase and precedes the place name البرز کوه (lit. ‘Alborz mountain’), a fixed name + noun order common in older Persian.
segments.s130-g18: که introduces the following clause causally/relatively (“for/because / where”); دیو is the subject ‘demon’, اندران (= اندر + ان) marks ‘among them / therein’; رنج‌ها is a plural object/phrase ‘the hardships/sufferings’; شد ستوه is a two-word predicate: شد (became) + ستوه (adjective ‘weary/of a sickening sort’), forming ‘became weary’.
segments.s130-g19: بفرمود is an honorific past reporting verb ‘he commanded/ordered’; کز (here ‘from which / from’) introduces the material phrase that follows: سنگ خارا ‘stone + flinty’ (خارا as adjective) and کنند is the 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/present (used after volitive verbs) meaning ‘they make’ — together: ‘he ordered that they make from the flinty rock’.
segments.s130-g20: دو خانه is the direct object ‘two houses’; برو here is an archaic/fused form (literally بر او = ‘on/for it’) introducing a clause about each house — functionally ‘of/for each’. هر یکی ‘each one’ repeats the distributive idea and is followed by the numeral ده ‘ten’ governing کمند ‘kamand’ (a device/name of coils/snare), so ‘each one ten kamands’. Note the classical ordering and the lack of an explicit copula.
segments.s130-g21: بیاراست is a past tense verb ‘he/she/they adorned / arranged’; آخر is an adverbial ‘at last / finally’; به + noun phrase indicates instrument or material here (به سنگ اندرون = ‘with stone for the interior’), with اندرون (inside/inner quarters) placed after the material noun — a typical poetic word order.
segments.s130-g22: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from / of’ marking material; پولاد میخ is an inverted genitive-like phrase ‘nails of steel’ (poetic order); و repeats the coordinate construction; ز خارا ستون likewise ‘pillars of flint’ (خارا here as noun ‘flint’). The repeated ز emphasizes material/means.
segments.s130-g23: ببستند is a past plural verb ‘they bound/tied/fastened’ (here ‘they harnessed’); اسپان is the old plural of اسب (‘horses’); جنگی is an adjective ‘warlike / for war’ modifying اسپان; بدوی is an attributive adjective (here a poetic epithet — literally ‘primitive/original’ but functioning as a descriptive epithet for the horses).
segments.s130-g24: هم is an additive particle ‘also’; اشتر here is treated as a proper name or individual camel/animal name; عماری‌کش is an epithet-name (literally ‘destroyer of buildings’) forming a compound name with اشتر; و connects the next compound راه جوی — راه ‘road/path’ + جوی ‘seeking/search’ yields a compound meaning ‘path‑finder’ (an epithet for an animal or creature that finds ways).
segments.s130-g33: This is a poetic, somewhat inverted clause. نبودی is the past negative copula 'was not' placed before the noun (verb–subject order is common in classical verse). تموز is the subject ('the month Tammuz/July'); ایچ پیدا is an emphatic negation phrase meaning 'not at all / nowhere apparent' (ایچ = 'at all', پیدا = 'visible/apparent'); ز دی gives the source/reference 'from the former/previous' (poetic 'from/of the former [time]'). Together: 'There was no sign of July at all (from the earlier time)'.
segments.s130-g34: Simple descriptive clause: هوا is the subject 'the air/atmosphere'; عنبرین is an adjectival form ('amber‑scented' or 'ambery') modifying it; بود is the past copula 'was'. The second half, بارانش می, omits the copula (a common poetic ellipsis): باران + possessive suffix -ش 'its rain' with می as the predicative noun 'wine' ('its rain was wine').
segments.s130-g35: به ایوانش is a locative phrase 'on/at its iwan' (به + ایوان + possessive -ش). یاقوت برده is a participial phrase: یاقوت 'rubies' + برده (past participle of بردن) 'brought/introduced'; بکار (= به کار) here expresses use/application — the compound implies the gems were brought to be used/set there ('brought and applied'). Note the participle/postpositive modifiers and the poetic order.
segments.s130-g36: ز پیروزه کرده is 'made/worked from turquoise' where ز marks material/source and کرده is the past participle 'made'. The phrase برو بر نگار indicates placement 'upon/over the painting/portrait' (بر = 'on'); in classical usage برو here is a fused/archaic locative/particle shaping the phrase 'بر نگار' ('on the picture'), so the whole segment says the turquoise work was placed upon the image. The clause again omits an explicit copula, relying on participial structure.
segments.s130-g37: همه‌ساله is an adverbial 'yearly/each year'. روشن بهاران forms the nominal phrase 'bright springs' (adjective before noun is frequent in poetry). بدی here functions as a past copula ('were/was') in an archaic/poetic form placed after the noun phrase — 'the springs were bright each year.' The exact morphological form بدی is classical; its sense as a copula is not listed in the provided senses, so it is treated as an archaic verb form.
segments.s130-g38: گلان 'flowers' is the subject; چون is the simile marker 'like/as'; رخ غمگساران is the comparison phrase 'the face/cheek of the sorrowful' (رخ = face/appearance, غمگساران = those who share sorrow / the sorrowful). Again بدی is the archaic past copula 'were' placed at the end. The line means 'the flowers were like the faces of those grieving.'
segments.s130-g39: This clause begins with a prepositional/source phrase: ز درد و غم و رنج 'from pain and sorrow and suffering' (ز lists the things the heart is removed from). دل is the theme 'the heart' and دور بود is the predicate 'was distant/removed' (دور بودن = to be free/at a distance from). Word order places the source phrase first and the copula بود at the end, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s130-g40: بدی را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker ('as for evil' or 'evil, (as for it)'); تن دیو is a noun phrase 'the body of the demon' (تن = body, دیو = demon); رنجور بود is the predicate 'was ailing/sickly' (رنجور = sickly/distressed, plus the past copula بود). The construction topicalizes 'بدی' and then states that the demon's body was afflicted.

sentences.s130-s21: Ill‑fate came into sleep from goodness and from the teacher of justice.
sentences.s130-s22: The court was seized by resentment; they were shaken by his wind‑tossed bosom (collar).
sentences.s130-s1: Kavus came toward Pars, and he then filled the world with fresh joy.
sentences.s130-s2: He adorned the throne and spread justice; into hearts he put joy and feasting.
sentences.s130-s3: He sent in every direction a champion — a sovereign, alert, and bright‑souled.
sentences.s130-s4: To Merv, Nishapur, Balkh, and Herat he sent troops, dispatching an army to every direction.
sentences.s130-s13: He built two other houses of glass and inlaid them throughout with peridot.
sentences.s130-s14: He made such a place for prancing and for feasting that the body might gain nourishment from the food.
sentences.s130-s15: He had two houses for war-weapons; he commanded that they be made from raw silver.
sentences.s130-s16: He built one golden palace for a seat; he raised it up and gave it two spans of height above.
sentences.s130-s5: The world became entirely full of justice; the wolf turned away from the lamb.
sentences.s130-s6: Because of so much treasure, beauty, and noble bearing, his path became beset with fairies, enemies, and snares.
sentences.s130-s7: Before Kavus the visitors were humbled; all his crown-bearers became an army.
sentences.s130-s8: He entrusted the world's heroism to Rostam; he counted all the days as good because of him.
sentences.s130-s9: He built a house on Mount Alborz, for a demon among them had become weary of the hardships.
sentences.s130-s10: He commanded that from the flinty rock they make two houses, each one with ten kamands.
sentences.s130-s11: Finally he fitted out the interior with stone — nails of steel and pillars of flint.
sentences.s130-s12: They harnessed/strapped war-horses, and also Ashtar Amari-kash and a path‑finder.
sentences.s130-s17: It was as if July did not exist at all; the air was perfumed with amber and its rain was wine.
sentences.s130-s18: On its iwan rubies were brought and set; turquoise had been worked and laid over the picture.
sentences.s130-s19: Each year the springs shone bright; the flowers were like the faces of the sorrowful.
sentences.s130-s20: The heart was free from pain, sorrow, and suffering; as for evil, the demon's body was ailing.
